event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a surreal, atmospheric depiction of a miniature cityscape bathed in cool blue and gray tones, accompanied by an ominous orchestral score, as a deep male voice speaks of "cutthroat competition and self-interest" and how one firm has become the "symbol of Wall Street." A translucent blue octopus emerges, its tentacles weaving through buildings including a White House model, symbolizing pervasive control, as golden text 'GOLDMAN SACHS' appears. Voices describe the firm as "much admired, much hated, and the best risk managers on Wall Street," with one noting it "cultivates ruthlessness" and that "for Goldman to be winners, there had to be losers." The scene shifts to a historical reenactment of New York in 1869 with period-dressed individuals on cobblestone streets, featuring sounds of horse-drawn carriages and distant city noise, under the text 'New York 1869.' The segment concludes with a close-up of a sepia-toned portrait of an elderly man with a white beard, as the authoritative voice returns, stating, "In 1886, after making a small fortune from his clothing business..." linking him to the firm's origins. The video then transitions to an earlier black-and-white office scene with Office Clerks engaged in clerical work, where a Jewish immigrant, Marcus Goldman, decides to enter finance by offering to buy small businesses' accounts receivables for cash, a practice that evolves into commercial papers, marking the beginning of the investment banking industry. This is contrasted with traditional commercial banks, as Goldman Sachs offers riskier financing. Marcus Goldman's business grows significantly, selling around $30 million in commercial papers annually by 1886, with a sepia-toned portrait of him signifying historical importance. The narrative continues with the introduction of Samuel Sachs as a partner, and by 1896, the firm, Goldman Sachs & Co., has $1.6 million in capital among five partners. Facing saturation in commercial paper trading, the firm seeks "the next big thing," which becomes "taking companies public," or IPOs, a concept explained by Prof. Robert Shiller at Yale. Goldman Sachs, initially an outsider as a 'Jewish firm,' aims to break into underwriting, strategizing to take a 'Jewish company public,' exemplified by Sears, Roebuck and Co.'s substantial financial growth, making it a prime IPO candidate. Goldman Sachs partners with Lehman Brothers for this undertaking, which proves to be a successful IPO, generating unprecedented fees for both firms. This success leads to many companies becoming clients, and by 1914, Goldman Sachs is a prominent investment bank. The senior partner, Henry Goldman, even contributes to the US Federal Reserve System's design. Recognizing IPOs as cyclical, Goldman Sachs seeks reinvestment strategies, coinciding with the prosperity of the "Roaring Twenties," described as a "go-go" period with widespread optimism and soaring markets. Neel Kashkari explains how Goldman Sachs, as an opportunist, capitalizes on this by forming a trading corporation, raising $93 million by selling shares to the public at a significantly higher price than their initial investment. This modern-day entity is compared to Special Purpose Acquisition Corporations (SPACs), highlighting that the "essence of finance hasn't really changed" as Goldman Sachs makes money "virtually from thin air" by creating investment trusts for which people are willing to pay.

Office Clerks.description: Several individuals in formal 19th- or early 20th-century attire, engaged in clerical work in a dimly lit office filled with wooden desks and stacks of bound documents.

Desk Clerk.description: A man in a suit seated at a large desk, reviewing documents, as another man in a suit approaches to hand him a paper.

Vintage City Street.description: A black-and-white aerial view of a busy city street from the early 20th century, filled with vintage automobiles, trolleys, and pedestrians, capturing the urban hustle of the era.

Goldman Sachs Trading Co. Building.description: A stylized, dark blue animated building with classical architecture, featuring a sign reading 'GOLDMAN SACHS TRADING CO.' above the entrance, shown at night with a glowing facade and a text overlay '$1M -> $10M' indicating financial growth, while a formal voice (Neel Kashkari) describes its formation with $1 million capital, subsequent sale of shares at 10 times the price, raising $93 million, and an original investment becoming worth almost $10 million. A subtle click/tap sound is heard during its animation.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a dark, atmospheric shot of a building entrance, quickly transitioning to a surreal miniature cityscape rendered in cool blue and gray tones. An ominous, low orchestral score begins as a deep, authoritative male voice narrates about "cutthroat competition and self-interest," and how "one firm has managed to survive for more than a century" to "become the symbol of Wall Street." Visually, a large, translucent blue octopus emerges from the fog, its tentacles weaving through the buildings, including a prominent White House model, symbolizing a powerful, pervasive force and control. Golden text 'GOLDMAN SACHS' appears over this misty scene. Another male voice describes the firm as "much admired, much hated, and the best risk managers on Wall Street," followed by a third voice stating it's "clearly a culture that cultivates ruthlessness." A fourth voice adds, "for Goldman to be winners, there had to be losers," all while the intense orchestral music builds. The scene then shifts to a historical reenactment of New York in 1869, with people in period clothing on cobblestone streets, accompanied by the environmental sounds of a horse-drawn carriage and distant city noise. The text 'New York 1869' is displayed. The segment concludes with a close-up of a sepia-toned portrait of an elderly man with a white beard, dressed in a formal suit, as the initial authoritative voice returns to narrate, "In 1886, after making a small fortune from his clothing business..." directly linking the man to the firm's origins.

The video opens with a black-and-white office scene from an earlier era, where several individuals in formal attire, the Office Clerks, are engaged in clerical work amidst stacks of bound documents and wooden desks. A man adjusts a robe on a mannequin while a woman interacts with another man at a desk. A consistent, slightly dramatic orchestral score plays as the narrator's clear voice begins, explaining that 'Jewish immigrant Marcus Goldman decides to get into finance,' seeing a need for small businesses to quickly turn their accounts receivables into cash. The scene transitions to a different office setting where three men, the Office Meeting Group, sit around a table with a vintage telephone and lamp; one man speaks animatedly while another listens intently, and a third man in the background appears to be writing. The narrator continues, detailing how 'bank loans are harder to get for these small businesses,' leading Goldman to offer to buy their short-term debt at a discount and resell it to other investors and bankers at a higher price. The next scene shows a man, the Desk Clerk, in a suit at a large desk, reviewing papers, as another man in a suit approaches and hands him a document. The video then cuts to a modern-day man, the Modern Speaker, in a checkered shirt speaking directly to the camera, with the text 'COMMERCIAL PAPERS' appearing in bold yellow letters, as the narrator explains that 'These short-term loans later became what's called commercial papers.' He adds that 'Around that time, it was the beginning of the investment banking industry,' contrasting it with commercial banks that provide 'guaranteed and safe financing,' while firms like Goldman Sachs provide 'riskier financing,' hence the term 'Investment Banks.' This is followed by a brief shot of a man in a suit and hat at a bank counter, the Bank Teller Scene, interacting with a teller behind bars, visually representing the traditional banking system. The video then returns to the Modern Speaker, who continues his narration, stating that 'Ten years later, Marcus Goldman grows his business to selling around $30 million in commercial papers per year.' The final sequence features a sepia-toned portrait of Marcus Goldman, the Historical Portrait, an elderly man with a white beard and mustache, wearing a formal suit, which is shown in close-up and then placed in a wooden frame on a wooden surface, suggesting reverence or historical significance, as the narrator concludes that 'At the age of 60, Goldman has already surpassed the average life expectancy.' The lighting throughout the historical scenes is dim and dramatic, while the modern scenes are well-lit and neutral, emphasizing a contrast between past and present.

The video opens with a close-up of two framed historical portraits, including a sepia-toned image of an elderly man with a white beard, as a male narrator, accompanied by tense, historical-sounding music, explains the need for the family business to thrive after retirement, leading to the introduction of his son-in-law, Samuel Sachs, as a partner. This transitions to an exterior night view of a stately building prominently displaying the golden text 'GOLDMAN SACHS & CO', while the narrator details how by 1896, Sachs had grown the firm to $1.6 million in capital, shared among five partners. The scene shifts to a black-and-white archival view of four men in suits gathered around a table, engaged in a serious discussion, with one reading a newspaper, as the narrator explains that the business of issuing and trading commercial papers is quickly becoming saturated. This is followed by a high-angle shot of a crowded early 20th-century street, where people gather around a building with a sign reading 'WATCH MYSTERY MAN CLIMB BUILDING AT 2 P.M. TODAY', as the narrator emphasizes the need to find "the next big thing" for growth. The video then cuts to a modern lecture setting at Yale, where Prof. Robert Shiller writes 'Investment Banking Underwriting' on a chalkboard, with the text 'INITIAL PUBLIC OFFERING' appearing on screen, while the narrator explains that by the late 19th century, the "next big thing" was "taking companies public," or "IPOs." A brief overlay of a vintage meeting with a woman presenting a chart appears before returning to Prof. Shiller, who now speaks directly, his voice clear as the background music fades, explaining that if it's the first time shares are issued, it's called an IPO, or "initial public offering." Prof. Shiller continues, elaborating that a private company, owned by just a few individuals, wishing to "go public" would typically approach an investment bank, which would then solve this problem by performing an "underwriting." The final shot is a black-and-white aerial view of a bustling early 20th-century city street, filled with vintage automobiles and pedestrians, as Prof. Shiller concludes his explanation.

The segment opens with a Modern Speaker, a man in a checkered shirt, seated in a modern office, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, narrating the historical context of IPOs and how Goldman Sachs, as a 'Jewish firm,' was an outsider determined to break into the underwriting business. As he speaks, a light, suspenseful background music begins to build. The scene transitions to a black-and-white historical setting, showing an Office Meeting Group of four men in suits around a round table, engaged in discussion, with one reading from a document while others smoke or listen intently, as the narrator explains Goldman Sachs's strategy to take a 'Jewish company public.' The narrative then shifts to an exterior shot of a large Sears, Roebuck and Co. building, with a sign advertising 'OPENS SATURDAY' and a crowd gathered, while the speaker details Sears's financial growth from a $250,000 net worth in 1897 to $37 million in revenue and $2.8 million in net income by 1905, calling it a 'perfect candidate for an IPO.' Inside the store, shoppers browse aisles filled with canned goods, with a woman selecting items, as a large yellow text overlay of '$2.8M' appears, directly correlating with the narrator's mention of Sears's net income. The speaker concludes by stating that taking Sears public would be a tremendous undertaking, leading Goldman Sachs to partner with another 'up-and-coming Jewish firm,' the Lehman Brothers, as the final scene shows a dark, blue-toned building facade at night with the 'LEHMAN BROTHERS' sign illuminated, and a single window glowing, suggesting a somber tone.

The video opens with a dark, blue-toned exterior shot of the Lehman Brothers building at night, its windows dark except for one faintly glowing, evoking a somber mood, as a clear male voice narrates financial details about "$30 million of common stocks" and "$10 million of preferred stocks for Sears." The scene transitions to a black screen with bold white text declaring 'THE IPO IS A SUCCESS!', accompanied by a subtle, serious orchestral background score, as the narrator explains how "both firms rake in an unprecedented amount of fees from this deal." Next, the camera shifts to the Goldman Sachs & Co. building, also at night, with a contemplative man in a suit on a bench outside, as the camera slowly zooms in on the golden 'GOLDMAN SACHS & CO' sign. The narrator continues, stating that "as a result of their success, many companies become their clients," and by "1914, Goldman Sachs becomes a prominent investment bank on Wall Street." The scene then cuts to a close-up of a framed portrait of a bald man with a mustache, dressed in formal attire, suggesting a figure of historical significance, as the narrator reveals that "the senior partner Henry Goldman even gets invited to become the designer for the US Federal Reserve System." The video then transitions to black-and-white archival footage, first showing a wide shot of the U.S. Capitol building, followed by a tense meeting of four men in suits around a round table in a vintage office, one speaking while others listen intently, as the narrator explains that "IPOs are a cyclical business" and Goldman Sachs "needs to find a way to reinvest its money." The scene shifts to a crowded, dimly lit social gathering with people in formal 1920s-30s attire mingling and drinking, representing the era of prosperity. The orchestral music fades, replaced by an upbeat, jazzy tune, as the narrator declares, "The timing is flawless." The final shot is a modern-day speaker in a dark suit and pink tie, gesturing emphatically in front of a blurred background of financial data, as an enthusiastic male voice proclaims, "The Roaring Twenties is one of the greatest bull markets in the history of America," followed by another exclaiming, "It was a boom like we've never had!"

The video opens with a contemporary financial speaker, identified as Jim Cramer, host of CNBC’s Mad Money, speaking directly to the camera in a modern studio. He reflects on a past era, stating there "was a belief that we were in the Emerald City," and describes the "roaring 20s" as a "go-go" period where "everybody was optimistic, markets were soaring," and it was "so easy for anybody to go get out a loan and then go speculate." This commentary overlays black-and-white archival footage of a bustling stock exchange floor, with traders shouting and gesturing amidst flashing numbers like '214' and '215'. The scene transitions to Neel Kashkari, President of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, who, in a formal office, introduces Goldman Sachs, explaining that "As an opportunist, Goldman Sachs intends to fully capitalize on it." A subtle click/tap sound accompanies the subsequent visual of a stylized, dark blue animated building labeled 'GOLDMAN SACHS & CO' at night. As the camera pans to the 'GOLDMAN SACHS TRADING CO.' sign with a '$1M -> $10M' text overlay, Kashkari details how it "forms a trading corporation by putting down $1 million in capital," then "immediately sells more shares to the public at 10 times the price," resulting in the corporation raising "$93 million" and their "original investment" becoming "worth almost $10 million." The narrative then shifts to a modern speaker in a checkered shirt, who explains that "Today the entity is more or less like the SPACs. Special Purpose Acquisition Corporations." He asserts that "the essence of finance hasn't really changed," even as he describes how "After making money virtually from thin air, Goldman Sachs starts more investment trusts, simply because there are people in the market willing to pay for them," before trailing off with "This is not unlike." This explanation is intercut with more black-and-white historical footage of a stock exchange ticker board displaying 'DOW JONES AVERAGES' and 'TODAY'S SALES', before returning to the modern speaker.

event_blocks.summary: Seated in a modern office, the Modern Speaker, wearing a checkered shirt, addresses the camera with animated hand gestures, drawing parallels between past economic bubbles like the 'dot.com bubble' and 'cryptocurrency mania' to the 'Roaring 20s,' where 'investment trusts' were conjured 'out of thin air.' A transition to a black-and-white historical montage features an archival male voice proclaiming a 'final triumph over poverty,' but the Modern Speaker counters that 'nothing good lasts forever.' This is underscored by visuals of a large crowd gathered before a building advertising a 'MYSTERY MAN CLIMB,' followed by a woman adjusting her wide-brimmed hat and a chaotic scrambling crowd, with discarded paper ribbons on the ground suggesting a failed event. The Modern Speaker announces the pivotal date, 'October 29th, 1929,' superimposed over a cityscape, signifying the stock market crash. A subsequent archival male voice, akin to a news announcer, details the dire consequences: 'inactive manpower... drifting helplessly in search of work,' a 22% decrease in marriages, a sharp decline in the birth rate, and a 40% higher suicide rate, visually reinforced by a truck laden with goods and a destitute group with children seated on the ground. The Modern Speaker stresses that 'the crash is the worst America has seen,' as a close-up of The New York Times reveals headlines detailing '90% DEPRECIATION' and 'Goldman Sachs Trading Off $289,566,330.' He concludes by stating that 'Goldman Sachs Trading Corporation is reduced almost to nothing,' with 'hundreds of millions of dollars evaporated,' and that the 'partnership of Goldman Sachs lost $13 million of its investment,' a financial devastation depicted by men in suits in a black-and-white office exchanging documents, accompanied by a stark '-$13M' overlay.

event_blocks.summary: An ominous hum accompanied a high-angle, black-and-white shot of a dense crowd gathered around a 'MORNINGTON' sign, setting a scene of public event as a narrator stated that hundreds of investment trusts went bust within a year, taking down investors and banks, but Goldman Sachs survived due to Sidney Weinberg. A dimly lit interior showed Weinberg conversing with two men, followed by a close-up of his framed portrait. The scene shifted to a modern street view of Red Hook, Brooklyn, with a 'RED HOOK LOBSTER POUND' sign and a passing van, while a young boy in a cap and apron in a kitchen received a paper from an older man, illustrating Weinberg's early life in a poor Brooklyn household where he quit high school to support his family, showing a willingness to work hard and network. The portrait of Weinberg reappeared, and a low-angle shot of someone mopping a wooden floor visually represented his humble beginnings as he "hustled his way to the very top of the company in a period of 15 years" starting as a janitor at Goldman Sachs. Charles D. Ellis, an author, commented on Weinberg's "tremendous capacity for connectivity with other people." The segment opened with a black-and-white close-up of a man in a suit at a desk reaching for a telephone, as a narrator detailed Sidney Weinberg's strategic ability to bring investment banking business to Goldman Sachs, transitioning to a wide shot of the 'GENERAL ELECTRIC' building, which Weinberg took public. A newspaper clipping with the headline 'SEC Charges Goldman Sachs Trading Corp. Controlled $1,694,494,103 Empire in 1929,' overlaid with a portrait of Walter S. Gresham, recounted the collapse of Goldman Sachs Trading Group and Sidney's clients' continued loyalty. The narrative shifted to a dimly lit, gritty urban underpass where people walked, including a boy in a cap and apron receiving a paper from an older man, followed by sweeping aerial views of industrial complexes with smokestacks, as the narrator described Weinberg's renewed ambition to take Ford Motors public. A title card 'FORD MOTOR CO. DEARBORN, MICHIGAN' appeared, leading into a brick factory building with a large mural and archival black-and-white footage of early 20th-century Model Ts driving out of a factory, as the narrator identified 'Ford Motors' as a giant car manufacturing company started by 'revolutionary entrepreneur, Henry Ford.' Douglas Brinkley, an author, provided expert commentary on Henry Ford's consumerism philosophy, explaining his focus on selling vehicles at their cheapest. Archival black-and-white footage of early 20th-century automobiles, including Model Ts, driving out of a factory, set Henry Ford's vision for "a car in every driveway." This historical industrial setting transitioned to a sweeping view of a massive industrial complex with smokestacks billowing smoke and a dramatic close-up of molten metal being poured, while the narrator explained that by 1956, Ford was the second largest car maker in America, and its IPO would make any investment bank a fortune. A framed portrait of Henry Ford was shown in a dimly lit brick room, followed by Douglas Brinkley, who explained that Henry Ford "never trusted Wall Street," viewing it as "gambling" and a way "to rip off the folks." The narrative highlighted Ford's controversial views with a historical portrait of him alongside a quote from 'The Dearborn Independent' stating, 'The Jew is a race that has no civilization to point to, no aspiring religion, no great achievement in any realm.' Donny Deutsch, former Chairman of Deutsch Inc., stated that Ford "was a great man but not a good man," and "was really a devout anti-Semite," emphasizing that "we can never mistake greatness in business for being a great person." The video showed black-and-white portraits of men in suits and a historical photograph of Walter S. Gresham, as the narrator mentioned JP Morgan Jr. failing to take Ford public. A bold title card declared, 'FORD WAS THE BIGGEST IPO IN HISTORY,' as the narrator noted that nine years after Henry Ford died, his grandson, Henry Ford II, was a "vastly different man," and for Sidney Weinberg, this was "his chance to cash in." Bill Cohan, an author, explained how Goldman Sachs underwrote Ford Motor Company's IPO in the 1950s, despite Henry Ford being considered anti-Semitic, marking a "sea change on Wall Street." A grainy, black-and-white close-up of hands counting stacks of cash next to a dollar sign on a cloth and handcuffs, as a narrator explained that as profits surged, Goldman Sachs sought new investment avenues. The scene shifted to a chaotic, vintage black-and-white shot of a crowded trading floor, likely the New York Stock Exchange, with traders in suits frantically moving and gesturing, while the narrator revealed that a smart trading department at Goldman Sachs was "making a killing." This was followed by a framed black-and-white portrait of a man in a suit and tie, placed on a wooden conference table in a library-like room, identified by the narrator as "legendary trader Gustav Levy." Historical black-and-white footage of a skyscraper under construction, with a worker climbing its steel frame, was shown as the narrator declared, "America is on the rise," experiencing rapid post-Civil War growth. This was followed by a street scene with vintage automobiles from the 1920s or 1930s, including a car with license plate '354-076', driving past a building adorned with American flags, while the narrator posed the question of which industry benefited most from this prosperity, answering, "Wall Street." The final sequence featured a modern, sleek glass building with the 'Goldman Sachs' logo prominently displayed, reflecting silhouettes of people walking by, as the narrator concluded that Goldman Sachs capitalized on expanding American businesses by taking them public, thereby making itself rich and powerful. The segment began with a close-up of a yellowed newspaper clipping, titled 'Ford Stock Set at $64.50; First Deals Drive Up Price' and featuring Louis P. Singer, detailing the Ford Motor Company's initial public offering. A male narrator explained that following the "IPO of GE and Ford Motors," Goldman Sachs became "one of the most profitable investment banks on Wall Street," adding, "But to them, it's never enough." Sumner Redstone, 'MAJORITY OWNER, VIACOM & CBS,' asserted, "They don't think in terms of money. They think in terms of winning... Your objective should be to win. To win, win, win all the time. Not sometimes, every time." The video returned to the Goldman Sachs building, followed by a black-and-white archival shot of a crowded, bustling trading floor, labeled 'BLOCK TRADING,' depicting intense financial activity, as the narrator explained that "One Goldman Sachs trader has struck gold with a trading strategy called Block Trading." The sequence continued with a close-up of a vintage rotary telephone, then a low-angle shot of a statue of a man on horseback in front of a grand, historic building. The narrator identified the trader as "Gus Levy," describing him as "Born in a middle-class household in May 1910 in New Orleans." A male narrator explains that "Within a year, hundreds of investment trusts go bust, taking down investors and investment banks with them." The scene cuts to a black screen displaying 'BUT GOLDMAN SACHS SURVIVED.', emphasizing resilience amid crisis. The hum continues as the narrator states, "Goldman Sachs manages to survive the crash, and its clients surprisingly stay with the firm. All thanks to one man. Sidney Weinberg." Visually, an elegant, dimly lit interior shows a bald, well-dressed man conversing with two standing men, followed by a close-up of a framed black-and-white portrait of a man in a pinstripe suit, glasses, and pocket square, which the audio identifies as Sidney Weinberg. A young boy in a cap and apron is then seen in a kitchen, receiving a piece of paper from an older man, visually suggesting mentorship, as the narrator details Sidney Weinberg's early life: "Sidney Weinberg is raised in a poor Brooklyn household. He's the third of 11 children. To support the family, he's forced to quit high school early. But even as a young man, Sidney shows a willingness to work hard and the ability to network." The portrait of the man, now with a nameplate reading 'PETER' (visually conflicting with the audio's "Sidney Weinberg"), reappears. A low-angle shot of someone mopping a wooden floor in a modern space visually represents Sidney's humble beginnings, as the narrator confirms, "Started as a janitor at Goldman Sachs. The segment opens with archival black-and-white footage of early 20th-century automobiles, including Model Ts, driving out of a factory, as an unidentified male voice states Henry Ford's vision for "a car in every driveway." This historical industrial setting transitions to a sweeping view of a massive industrial complex with smokestacks billowing smoke and a dramatic close-up of molten metal being poured, while the narrator explains that by 1956, Ford was the second largest car maker in America, and its IPO would make any investment bank a fortune. A framed portrait of Henry Ford is shown in a dimly lit brick room, followed by Douglas Brinkley, an author, who appears in a sophisticated room and audibly explains that Henry Ford "never trusted Wall Street," viewing it as "gambling" and a way "to rip off the folks." The narrative then highlights Ford's controversial views with a historical portrait of him alongside a quote from 'The Dearborn Independent' stating, 'The Jew is a race that has no civilization to point to, no aspiring religion, no great achievement in any realm.' Donny Deutsch, former Chairman of Deutsch Inc., appears next in a modern, softly lit room, audibly stating that Ford "was a great man but not a good man," and "was really a devout anti-Semite," emphasizing that "we can never mistake greatness in business for being a great person." The video shows black-and-white portraits of men in suits and a historical photograph of Walter S. Gresham, as the narrator mentions JP Morgan Jr. failing to take Ford public. A bold title card declares, 'FORD WAS THE BIGGEST IPO IN HISTORY,' as the narrator notes that nine years after Henry Ford died, his grandson, Henry Ford II, was a "vastly different man," and for Sidney Weinberg, this was "his chance to cash in." The segment concludes with Bill Cohan, an author, appearing on a laptop screen in a modern office, as upbeat, slightly tense industrial-sounding music plays, and the narrator mentions a "big breakthrough."

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a close-up of a framed portrait and a vintage rotary telephone, as a narrator describes a life dedicated to business. The scene shifts to another portrait on a dark wall, accompanied by 'FINANCE MAGAZINE OF MONEY' text, while the narrator explains how 'Gus Levy' expanded the trading business for Goldman Sachs. As the camera pans up a modern glass skyscraper at dusk, the narrator details how increased securities issuance and larger-scale trading made the firm vulnerable. A man in a modern office then states that finance companies are 'one mistake away from a total collapse,' a sentiment echoed by the narrator's declaration that 'Goldman Sachs is back to make that mistake.' The scene transitions to the '200 WEST STRE' building entrance, then to a bustling Penn Station with train sounds. Vintage footage shows a Penn Central train, number '3107', pulling a teal freight car through snow, as the narrator explains the 1968 merger of the Pennsylvania Railroad and New York Central Railroad into Penn Central, the country's largest railroad company. Train sounds continue over archival footage of a crowded street overlaid with '$1.2B' text, linked by the narrator to the company's existing debt. The segment concludes with a bank counter scene featuring '$100M' text, as the narrator notes the company's need for operating funds and its hiring of Goldman Sachs for issuance, accompanied by intensifying music. The video then shows a formal dinner with men in suits, overlaid with '$100M' text, representing 'another $100 million of commercial papers' with a high rating underwritten by Goldman Sachs, leading investors to believe Penn Central's papers were safe. A dark screen with 'BUT THEY WERE WRONG.' appears, followed by an aerial view of a rail yard. A yellowed New York Times clipping from May 1, 1974, headlined 'S.E.C. Files Penn Central Fraud Suit,' is shown, as an older male voice reports on Penn Central's failure to generate revenue, leading to track and equipment deterioration. A modern speaker in an office explains that after Penn Central's bankruptcy, investors sued Goldman Sachs for misrepresentation. Another New York Times clipping from October 10, 1974, headlined 'GOLDMAN, SACHS LOSES RAIL CASE,' is displayed, as the speaker elaborates on the irony of a national credit office giving debt a high rating due to Goldman Sachs' underwriting, which then prompted the company to issue more debt based on these ratings.

event_blocks.summary: A melancholic piano melody accompanied the Boy in Kitchen as he stirred ingredients like lemons, tomatoes, and garlic in a dimly lit kitchen, the subtle clinking of utensils audible before an abrupt cut to black. A formal address followed, featuring a man in a suit speaking at a podium with microphones. Introducing Gus Levy, a narrator stated, "For a while, Gus Levy seemed invincible," as a newspaper clipping headlined 'Rockefeller Eulogizes Gustave Levy Of Wall Street as 'Fabulous Friend'' appeared, displaying Gus Levy's portrait and biographical details. The narrator explained Gus Levy "transformed Goldman Sachs from a boutique investment banking house to an elite institution," while an image of Gus Levy with his years '1910–1976' was shown against a grand building, succeeded by a sepia-toned photograph of him smiling with two companions. The narrative shifted to a somber tone as the narrator revealed, "But in recent years, the stress may have caught up to him. He dies from a stroke during a business trip," adding, "It's a shock to everyone at the firm, as Gus Levy never even left any succession plan." The piano music intensified as the narrator announced, "But people at Goldman Sachs know there is one man who's capable for the job. And he may just be the guy to turn Goldman Sachs around," naming "John Whitehead." This coincided with a dramatic black-and-white naval battle scene of warships firing amidst smoke and a loud explosion, overlaid with a portrait of a man with light hair. The narrator identified him as "Harvard educated, John Whitehead was also a Navy commander during World War II and was a part of the D-Day," before the audio cut off, briefly showing a close-up of a gray-haired man with intense eyes looking off-camera.

event_blocks.summary: The video commences with a sepia-toned military montage, featuring soldiers in a tank, charging through water, and naval vessels, accompanied by an authoritative male voice asserting that business must be conducted "the way has been" and that "the firm needs to operate like the military, where everyone must follow a code of conduct." This transitions to a well-lit office where Anthony Scaramucci speaks to the camera, followed by a reflective male voice questioning how new hires can be "indoctrinated with the principles of Goldman Sachs" and expected conduct, as a close-up reveals a document titled 'Our Business Principles' on a yellow folder, highlighting client interests, teamwork, and ethical conduct. The voice explains he "wrote down what turned out to be these principles" on a yellow pad on a Sunday afternoon. A black-and-white office scene shows a man writing on a whiteboard while another listens, as the authoritative voice returns, stating "Whitehead also starts to standardize the investment banking business at Goldman, making sure the employees put their clients interest first." The narrative shifts back to Scaramucci in a warm-lit office with wooden furnishings, energetically recounting a story about waiting five hours for an important person who arrived at 10 PM on a Friday before a holiday weekend, with the lesson being "waiting for people that are more important than you." Brief shots of a modern office lounge with yellow floral arrangements and a dimly lit airport terminal with travelers are interspersed. The segment concludes with Scaramucci reinforcing themes of corporate culture and leadership through his gestures and commentary.

event_blocks.summary: The 'kamikoto/神等' brand is promoted with a world map highlighting endorsing chefs, emphasizing the 'unbelievably sharp' Japanese steel knives, individually inspected with a lifetime guarantee, presented in a 'heavy-duty ashwood box' alongside a 'Certificate of Authenticity.' A modern speaker in a blue and white checkered shirt offers a '$50 discount' using coupon code 'FINAIUS50.' The scene shifts to a vibrant 1980s New York street parade with dancers, crowds, flags, and vintage cars, as a voice describes the era as a 'decade of deal-making' with 'easy money and relaxed regulation' fostering mergers and acquisitions, concluding with a shot of a modern glass building displaying the 'Goldman Sachs' logo. In 1983, under John Whitehead, Goldman Sachs achieved $400 million in profit, a 60% increase. Upon Whitehead's retirement to become Deputy Secretary of State, Steve Freeman and Robert Rubin took over; Rubin built the options trading business into a major profit engine. The narrative then focuses on Robert M. Freeman, a Goldman Sachs senior partner arrested in 1987 for insider trading, highlighted by a newspaper clipping. The segment details a 'hostile takeover deal over the broadcasting company Storer' for '$2.1 Billion Kohlberg Deal,' linking Freeman's indirect revelation of a call option sale price to a business friend. The modern speaker calls this 'classic insider trading,' noting that while such incidents often go unnoticed, 'not this time,' as Robert Freeman's actions were noticed by rising politician Rudy Giuliani, the US Attorney for the Southern District of New York. Giuliani, seeking to create a 'public spectacle,' deliberately prolonged the case and leaked information to the media, while Freeman pleaded guilty a year after Giuliani ran for mayor in 1989. The segment concludes with a shot of 'Mr. Bob Rubin' seated at a formal table.

event_blocks.summary: Seated in an orange armchair, Mr. Bob Rubin clasped his hands as somber music began, while a narrator explained how Goldman Sachs' reputation was "dragged through the mud." The scene shifted to a black-and-white office where two men shook hands amidst framed certificates and an 'AFRICAN' poster, as the narrator detailed clients' continued trust in Goldman Sachs due to their ability to "bounce back from crises" and focus on "making them money." Stan Jonas of FIMAT Brokers, described as a bald man with a gray beard, is shown speaking in an office, explaining, "In a moment of tremendous clarity, they realized that two risky positions taken together can effectively eliminate risk itself." The narrative continues with a man walking confidently and a modern speaker in a checkered shirt gesturing, as the narrator details the hedge fund's "instant success, making 40% during its first year," and the competition among Wall Street firms to become its trading partner. event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a calm male voice explaining financial models designed by economists studying market history, accompanied by tense background music, while a computer monitor displays a man in a suit and tie. A transition to a black screen with gold text 'BUT THEY FORGET ONE RULE......' precedes Leo Melamed of the Chicago Mercantile Exchange stating, "That's an old market rule. Uh, the market will test you and do what you don't expect it to do." An aerial view of Moscow, including Red Square and St. Basil’s Cathedral, is shown as the narrator describes the "so-called Black Swan event" of 1998, when a sovereign country defaulted on its debt, a scenario not accounted for by financial models. An older man with white hair sips wine in a dimly lit room as a British-accented female voice narrates the unexpected default of a major country on its international debts in August 1998. The scene shifts to a chaotic, high-angle shot of a crowded trading floor with '-$353M' overlaid, where the narrator reports, "In a single day, LCTM loses $353 million," as the music intensifies. The segment concludes with a modern trading desk displaying 'THE OPENING BELL' and a 'Goldman Sachs' sign, where traders work amidst the narrator's statement that "After learning about LCTM's trouble, Goldman Sachs immediately starts dumping its own bond positions into the market, accelerating..." The video then shifts to a close-up of a man at a high-tech trading desk with a 'Goldman Sachs' sign, as the narrator discusses "LCTM's downfall" and the Fed's bailout of LTCM, which also saved Goldman Sachs, illustrated by a shot of the Federal Reserve Building. An aerial view of Manhattan is followed by archival footage of Rep. Bernie Sanders questioning the "three and a half billion dollar bailout for billionaires" and "gambling practices of the Wall Street elites." After quick cuts of a city street, a modern trading floor, and a vintage trading floor, the narrator reiterates that "LTCM's failure may have almost caused a financial crisis, but for Goldman Sachs, it only matters that Goldman Sachs survives." As tense music builds, a modern skyscraper, a contemporary trading floor with 'NYSE' visible, and the New York Stock Exchange facade are shown. The narrator announces, "Now, with the LTCM's crisis resolved, and at the peak of the dot-com bubble, Goldman Sachs goes public," followed by a news anchor reporting on the "big debut expected tomorrow for the Goldman Sachs initial public offering," and concluding with archival footage of men celebrating, an American flag, and a ticker board, confirming, "Goldman Sachs goes public by the end of 1999, selling 11..." The narrative continues with a historical black-and-white clip of men celebrating, overlaid with details of Goldman Sachs' $3.7 billion IPO offering at $53 per share for 69 million shares (ticker GS), as Charles Morris explains the IPO brought in significant capital and required the release of previously unseen financial information. A Modern Speaker (Checkered Shirt) in a dimly lit office discusses Goldman Sachs' pre-tax profit of $12.2 billion in the five and a half years before 1998, much of which was paid to partners. The Goldman Sachs building facade is shown, emphasizing the '$12.2B' profit. As a public company, Goldman Sachs sought new revenue streams, leading to a boom in mortgage-backed securities by 2000, visually represented by men celebrating in an office, followed by images of Larry Merlo, a suburban house with a 'FOR SALE' sign, and an energetic crowd cheering. An aerial view of a suburban neighborhood appears, with Charles Morris commenting on people seeking "their very own piece of the American Dream," and a different male voice describes a "huge boom in housing that made no sense at all." The segment opens with an older man speaking against a forest backdrop, stating that "the appetites of the financial sector drove what everybody else did," accompanied by dramatic music. A red bar chart titled 'SUBPRIME LOANS (AS % OF ALL LOANS)' shows a rise from approximately 8% in 2002 to nearly 20% by 2006, as the narrator explains that "By 2006, mortgage-backed securities become one of the most profitable businesses for Wall Street banks." A city street scene at dusk with a modern glass building and American flags is overlaid with '$203 B,' as the narrator specifies, "Goldman Sachs underwrites about $203 billion of mortgage-backed securities." A framed portrait of Hank Paulson is shown, and the narrator states, "At the peak of his success, Hank Paulson follows Goldman Sachs tradition." Archival footage shows President George W. Bush nominating Henry Paulson as Secretary of the Treasury, praising his business experience and financial knowledge. Henry Paulson is then seen smiling in an outdoor urban setting. The narrator announces, "Since trading has become the main business for Goldman Sachs, the next CEO will be a trader. Lloyd Blankfein." The final segment features a man in a checkered shirt speaking directly to the camera, as the narrator concludes, "At that point, Goldman Sachs is almost like a hedge fund. They have tens of billions of their own money managed..."

The video cuts to an aerial daytime view of Moscow, with 'MOSCOW' overlaid, showcasing Red Square and St. Basil’s Cathedral, as the first male voice explains, "In 1998, the so-called Black Swan event happened. Something that their models didn't account for." This shifts to a nighttime shot of the illuminated cathedral, while the voice continues, "A sovereign country defaulting on its debt." The scene changes to an older man with white hair sipping from a wine glass in a dimly lit room, as a calm female voice with a British accent narrates, "August 1998. Something happened no one had considered possible. The biggest country in the world, suddenly and without explanation, refused to pay all its international debts." This is followed by a chaotic, high-angle shot of a crowded trading floor, overlaid with '-$353M', while the first male voice states, "In a single day, LCTM loses $353 million," with the music intensifying. The final scene shows a modern trading desk with multiple monitors, one displaying 'THE OPENING BELL', and a 'Goldman Sachs' sign, as traders work in a dimly lit environment, and the male voice concludes, "After learning about LCTM's trouble, Goldman Sachs immediately starts dumping its own bond positions into the market, accelerating..." The music continues to build.

The segment opens with a close-up of a man from behind at a high-tech trading desk, surrounded by monitors, with a 'Goldman Sachs' sign visible on the wall, as a male narrator discusses "LCTM's downfall." The narrator continues, explaining how "the Fed comes in and bails out LCTM, and saving Goldman Sachs in the process," while the scene transitions to an exterior shot of the grand Federal Reserve Building. An aerial view of a dense urban skyline, likely Manhattan, then gives way to a grainy archival close-up of Rep. Bernie Sanders, who, with a C-SPAN2 graphic, questions why "the Federal Reserve has organized the three and a half billion dollar bailout for billionaires," and why "Americans should be worried about the gambling practices of the Wall Street elites." The scene transitions through a narrow city street, a bustling modern trading floor with an agitated trader, and a chaotic vintage trading floor, while the narrator reiterates that "LTCM's failure may have almost caused a financial crisis, but for Goldman Sachs, it only matters that Goldman Sachs survives." Tense instrumental music begins to build as a modern glass skyscraper is shown, followed by a contemporary trading floor with 'NYSE' visible on a monitor, and then the iconic New York Stock Exchange facade. The narrator announces, "Now, with the LTCM's crisis resolved, and at the peak of the dot-com bubble, Goldman Sachs goes public," as the music swells dramatically. A news anchor in a studio, with a graphic about 'THE DOW'S NINTH WORST YEAR WAS 1932', reports on "A big debut expected tomorrow for the Goldman Sachs initial public offering." The segment concludes with a black-and-white archival image of men clapping and smiling, an American flag and ticker board in the background, as the narrator confirms, "Goldman Sachs goes public by the end of 1999, selling 11..." before the audio cuts off.

The video opens with a historical black-and-white clip of men in suits celebrating, overlaid with text detailing Goldman Sachs' $3.7 billion IPO offering at $53 per share for 69 million shares (ticker GS). A male narrator, later identified as Charles Morris, explains that this IPO brought in $3.7 billion and required Goldman Sachs to release a lot of never-before-seen information, revealing how profitable the company had been. The scene transitions to a Modern Speaker (Checkered Shirt) seated in a dimly lit office, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, as the narrator continues, detailing Goldman Sachs' pre-tax profit of $12.2 billion in the five and a half years before 1998, much of which was paid out to partners due to partnership accounting. The Goldman Sachs building facade then appears, with the company name in white letters on glass, followed by a bold green text overlay of '$12.2B', emphasizing the financial magnitude. The narrator states that as a public company, Goldman Sachs searched for more ways to make money, leading to a new business booming by the start of 2000: mortgage-backed securities. This narrative is visually accompanied by a group of men in a modern office celebrating with raised fists and laughter, suggesting a successful deal, followed by a thoughtful Larry Merlo, a suburban house with a 'FOR SALE' sign, and an energetic crowd cheering and raising their arms in a modern room, with accompanying sounds of crowd cheering and applause. An aerial view of a suburban neighborhood appears, with the text 'Charles Morris | Author' overlaid, as Charles Morris's voice comments on people coming to hear how to buy "their very own piece of the American Dream." The final shot is another aerial view of a similar neighborhood, reinforcing the theme of suburban development, as a different male voice describes a "huge boom in housing that made no sense at all," before the audio cuts off. Throughout, a driving background music track underscores the narrative, becoming more prominent during the crowd scenes.

The video opens with a close-up of an older man with white hair, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking seriously against a stylized black-and-white forest backdrop, stating that the 'appetites of the financial sector drove what everybody else did,' accompanied by a dramatic underscore. The scene transitions to a dark red bar chart titled 'SUBPRIME LOANS (AS % OF ALL LOANS)', visually demonstrating a steady increase from approximately 8% in 2002 to nearly 20% by 2006. A narrator explains that 'By 2006, mortgage-backed securities become one of the most profitable businesses for Wall Street banks.' The chart fades into a city street scene at dusk, featuring a modern glass building, American flags, and traffic lights, with the bold yellow text '$203 B' superimposed, as the narrator specifies that 'Goldman Sachs underwrites about $203 billion of mortgage-backed securities.' The next shot reveals a framed portrait of a bald man in a light blue shirt and orange tie, standing with hands in pockets, displayed in a modern office setting. The narrator then states, 'At the peak of his success, Hank Paulson follows Goldman Sachs tradition,' as archival footage shows President George W. Bush at a podium in the White House Rose Garden, flanked by two men. President Bush announces, 'I'm pleased to announce that I will nominate Henry Paulson to be the Secretary of the Treasury,' praising his 'lifetime of business experience,' 'intimate knowledge of financial markets,' and 'ability to explain economic issues in clear terms.' The scene shifts to a modern outdoor urban setting where Henry Paulson, a bald man in a suit and red patterned tie, smiles while conversing with a woman in a dark suit, with construction barriers and city buildings in the background. The narrator continues, 'Since trading has become the main business for Goldman Sachs, the next CEO will be a trader. Lloyd Blankfein.' The final segment features a man in a blue and white checkered shirt, seated in a dimly lit office, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, as the narrator concludes, 'At that point, Goldman Sachs is almost like a hedge fund. They have tens of billions of their own money managed...'

event_blocks.summary: In a modern office, a Modern Speaker (Checkered Shirt) calmly reflected on clients facing the "biggest financial storm in recent history" amidst a dramatic orchestral score. The scene shifted to a bustling, dimly lit trading floor, then an exterior view of a New York City cityscape featuring an American Flag and an 'NYC 125' banner, with '2007' superimposed over an aerial Manhattan view. A news report commenced with a male voice announcing "The new story, the mortgage meltdown." A female news anchor elaborated on the "mortgage meltdown," noting a "record number of Americans are losing their homes." A male anchor in a pinstripe suit emphasized that "more than 70 mortgage companies have failed" and "the biggest of the big companies, the Bear Stearns, the big banks are caught holding the bag," as 'MORTGAGE MELTDOWN' and 'YOUR $$$$' flashed on screen. A close-up of a 'BEAR STEARNS' sign was followed by sweeping aerial views of a City Skyline across a river. The initial speaker's voice returned, stating that a once "profitable business turns out to be a financial weapon of massive destruction." The segment concluded with Nouriel Roubini | NYU, speaking seriously with a distinct accent about the "securitization food chain," explaining that participants "didn't care about the quality of the mortgage" but focused on "maximizing the volume and getting a fee out of it," as the Securitization Food Chain Graphic visually outlined this complex financial structure.

event_blocks.summary: The financial crisis of September 16, 2008, saw the "fire sale of Bear Stearns to JP Morgan Chase" and the bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers, pushing the investment banking sector towards ruin, with Goldman Sachs identified as particularly vulnerable due to its exposure to subprime mortgage loans, prompting President George W. Bush to warn of a potential "global meltdown." Amidst this turmoil, Warren Buffett emerged as an "unlikely savior" for Goldman Sachs, investing in the firm and providing it with crucial breathing room, as evidenced by a New York Times article highlighting the "Buffett Deal at Goldman Seen as a Sign of Confidence." The crisis also brought to light the complex and controversial world of financial derivatives, specifically "credit default swaps," with author Bill Cohan explaining Goldman Sachs' early recognition of the impending trouble and their "big proprietary bet against the mortgage market." A formal hearing featured "MR. LEVIN CHAIRMAN" decrying the practice of "selling junk" and "betting against your own customer," while a Goldman Sachs representative defended their business model, stating they would "create" any derivative a client desired, though asserting the "trading department are separate." News headlines later detailed a "2008 scandal" that marked the "beginning of Goldman Sachs' deteriorating reputation," involving money laundering probes, SEC settlements, political crises, and significant investment losses, suggesting "multiple illegal activities." Despite these controversies, Goldman Sachs was perceived as "exceptionally good at spotting money-making trends" and executing them effectively, quickly cutting losses and bouncing back, consistently being among the first to capitalize on market booms. By 2018, with the rise of retail investors and companies like Robinhood offering free commission trading, a "booming market" emerged, driving the growth of consumer finance, which Goldman Sachs viewed as a "new opportunity to cash in," leading to reports of the firm offering wealth management services "for the masses."

CNBC Newsline.description: A news graphic featuring a photo of Warren Buffett and a breaking news ticker stating 'BUFFETT: WAS WARY OF LEHMAN'S VALUATIONS OF ASSETS WHEN APPROACHED EARLIER THIS YEAR', visually reinforcing the narrator's discussion of Buffett's role.

I do know that the American economy over a period of time will do very well and people that want a piece of it will do well.'

The scene transitions to the JPMorgan Chase & Co. building, where a man carries stacked boxes down steps, with the date 'September 16, 2008' overlaid, as the narrator continues, "and the bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers. The entire investment banking sector is on the verge of total ruin." Tense, dramatic music underscores the narrative. The camera then moves to the Goldman Sachs building, its name prominently displayed, as the narrator states that "Having a large exposure to subprime mortgage loans, Goldman Sachs is among the most vulnerable firms on Wall Street." President George W. Bush is then seen speaking at a podium, his expression serious, while the narrator declares, "We are faced with the prospect of a global meltdown." The narrator continues, "Out of options, Goldman Sachs turns to an unlikely savior," as the scene shifts to a black-and-white office where Warren Buffett smiles warmly. The dramatic music intensifies. A CNBC Newsline graphic appears, featuring Buffett and a ticker about his wariness of Lehman's valuations. President George W. Bush's voice is then heard, stating, "I have no idea what the stock market's going to do next month or six months from now. I do know that the American economy over a period of time will do very well and people that want a piece of it will do well." This audio plays over a New York Times article titled 'Buffett Deal at Goldman Seen as a Sign of Confidence,' featuring a photo of Buffett and Goldman Sachs CEO Lloyd Blankfein laughing. The narrator then explains, "Warren Buffett's investment gives Goldman Sachs some breathing room." The final scene shows a dimly lit trading floor where a bald man in a suit speaks urgently on the phone amidst monitors and a chaotic environment, as the narrator concludes, "To recoup from the trading losses, Goldman Sachs not only unloads the mortgage-related assets..."

The minute opens with a tense scene on a dimly lit trading floor, where a bald man in a dark suit urgently speaks on a phone amidst monitors, as a male voice discusses financial derivatives, specifically "credit default swaps," and the strategy of "betting against them." This financial theme is visually reinforced by bold yellow text 'CREDIT DEFAULT SWAPS' overlaid on a modern skyscraper. Author Bill Cohan, seen in a well-lit office, then explains how Goldman Sachs "figured out before other firms that trouble was coming," making a "big proprietary bet against the mortgage market" in December 2006. The scene shifts to a nighttime aerial view of the U.S. Capitol Building, leading to a close-up of a formal hearing where 'MR. LEVIN CHAIRMAN' sternly declares that one "shouldn't be selling junk," "crap," or "betting against your own customer at the same time you're selling to them." In a modern office meeting room with the 'Goldman Sachs' logo visible, a man in a checkered shirt animatedly argues that if a client wants a derivative, Goldman Sachs "will create it for him," but the actions of their "trading department are separate." The segment concludes with a close-up of the 'Goldman Sachs' logo on a dark marble wall, followed by a screen displaying news headlines detailing the firm's controversies, including money laundering probes, SEC settlements, political crises, and significant investment losses, as a male voice states that the "2008 scandal is just the beginning of Goldman Sachs' deteriorating reputation" and their involvement in "multiple illegal activities." Throughout, a tense, dramatic background score underscores the gravity of the financial discussions and revelations.

The minute opens with an ominous, low-frequency background music as a dusk street scene outside a modern glass building shows pedestrians, including a man in a light blue shirt and a woman in a white tank top, walking past before the camera pans to reveal the illuminated interior and the 'ZARA' logo above the entrance. The scene transitions indoors to a man in a blue and white checkered shirt, seated in a dark leather chair, speaking expressively with hand gestures. He states, "Even with all of that, Goldman Sachs emerges unscathed and his clients are still with the firm," continuing, "I think the secret to Goldman Sachs' success is that it's exceptionally good at spotting money-making trends and simply executing it better than others. And when the bubble burst, they're quick to cut losses and bounce back." He concludes, "Whenever there's a boom, Goldman Sachs is always among the first to ride it." The video then cuts to a bright, sunlit urban street lined with classical and modern skyscrapers, including a building with American flags, as the male voice continues, "By 2018, America finds itself on the precipice of another booming market, driven by retail investors." This shifts to a celebratory moment at the Nasdaq, where a diverse group, including a woman in a white dress and a man in a white suit, cheer and clap behind a podium labeled 'HOOD Nasdaq Listed' with 'Robinhood' and 'Welcome to the new Wall Street' displayed on green screens. The camera then tilts upward to show towering digital billboards in Times Square, all displaying Robinhood’s green branding and Nasdaq listings, as the speaker explains, "As companies like Robinhood offer free commission trading, retail investors are flooding the market, driving the growth of consumer finance." The scene changes to a blue-lit building with a 'GOLDMAN SACHS' sign, while the male voice states, "Goldman Sachs sees this as a new opportunity to cash in." Finally, a CNBC news anchor with blonde hair, wearing a dark top, reports from a studio with a 'CLOSING BELL' graphic and a lower-third banner reading 'GOLDMAN SACHS TO OFFER WEALTH MANAGEMENT FOR THE MASSES', as she begins, "2020 has seen the rise of the retail..."

event_blocks.summary: A news anchor with long blonde hair, under the CNBC 'CLOSING BELL' banner, reports that Goldman Sachs, traditionally serving the affluent, is now extending wealth management services to the general public. This announcement is followed by an animated sequence depicting a family silhouette, a house, and mountains, accompanied by a calm female voice stating, 'Investing can be an important part of creating the life you want,' culminating in the 'Marcus: by Goldman Sachs' logo and a male voice introducing 'Marcus Invest, an automated way to invest from Goldman Sachs,' all set to gentle, uplifting background music. The scene then shifts to real-world footage of a modern urban environment, focusing on the '200 WEST STREET' address above the entrance of a sleek glass building, identified as a Goldman Sachs office, with American flags waving. Over this, a deep, authoritative male voice remarks, 'It is now 2022, and the world has changed. But for Goldman Sachs, it's still the same hungry, opportunistic company.' An older, gravelly male voice praises the firm's 'formidable internal culture of commitment and teamwork.' The video concludes with a dramatic, darkly lit animation of a large, translucent blue octopus with textured skin moving its tentacles through a miniature cityscape, as the authoritative male voice declares, 'In a game of the survival of the fittest, Goldman Sachs has managed to outlast the majority,' followed by the emergence of the 'GOLDMAN SACHS' golden text. The older male voice adds, 'It's a firm that is highly disciplined and efficient for one thing... Profit,' while moody, cinematic music with fog and low lighting intensifies with a low hum and subtle percussion, ending resonantly.
